it was a beautiful film, period. was it an accurate recreation of the original text? no. that’s why the title is in quotation marks. we should be able to distinguish between two artistic works and give appreciation to each. if this was marketed to be an exact interpretive film, sure, we can be upset and raise our eyebrows, but it seemed very clear to me going in that this was how the book made fennell feel when she read it, not an exact recreation. the acting was good, cinematography was unreal, it pulled me in, brought up emotions, stirred discussion and led me to want to reread wuthering heights. if you can’t see past this just being a "sex film" then you should really evaluate the deeper themes. why is this being demonized? there are further discussions to be had on casting choices, yes. however overall i still would interpret this as a very solid film without the stupid netflix lighting, something we don’t get often. maybe an unpopular opinion but i recommend watching open minded.

Beautiful movie, both visually and due to the twisted and tragic love story between Cathy and Heathcliff.  The costuming alone deserves an Oscar from my humble opinion. The one note that I have is regarding the character of Heathcliff as an adult, I didnt quite understand how he became well to do and it wasnt explained very well in the film. I also thought that they made the look of his character, a little too haggard looking with the gold tooth, cane, and disheveled hair.  Lastly, I know its acting, but it did throw me a little bit knowing that an English period piece was being led by two Aussie actors.  The accents were okay but not great. The highlight for me were the children playing the younger versions. The performances were heartfelt touching, and I cried thinking about their scenes in the firm for days following.  So touching. What was off putting and brought down my rating was the costume, music and art direction the directors overuse of arty scene made the move much longer than need be. 

So of the costume lost the immersion as they had the feel of Goth Barbie meets my little ponysorry there was no iridescent plastic fabric back then.

The sets and environment too had me thinking they were in a Tim Burton, Beatle Juice version of Charls Dickson England and I expected a sand-worm to pop up or Ebenezer with braces to appear.

Only half the music fit as it was timeless orchestra, but the modern contemporary, again lost the immersion.

In short, those who dont know the book will enjoy the torrid love story, or character (including some left out of this version) along with those who know nothing of the period wont notice the strange modern plugs the director made.

This adaptation of WUTHERING HEIGHTS is really a different story with different characters than in the novel, which changes a story that was fundamentally about the cycle of child abuse into a run-of-the mill period love triangle. Catherine is no longer a borderline persionality, Heathcliff is transformed into a Romantic Hero instead of a cruel Byronic one, and Isabella is a quirky, mousey young woman instead of smart and beautiful but tragically naive. Cut from the film is the entire second half of the novel, including Catherine's daughter, Cathy, and Heathcliff and Isalbella's son Linton, and their love affair and the abuse they suffer at the hands of Heathcliff. While this could be considered an average film if it didn't claim to be an adaptation of WUTHERING HEIGHTS, seeing one of my favorite novels desicrated in this manner made we want to hurl

I was extremely satisfied with the movie and loved the departure It took from the book. I’m a big Margot Robbie fan and have been watching Jacob Elordi’s career just skyrocket with great film after great limited series after great show… All of them playing characters vastly different and complex. I thought that, given where Emerald Fennel took the movie, it made the casting make more sense. This was definitely not an adaptation, just as she said it would not be. But it was a true love letter to the themes of the book without falling into the ridiculously long line of film and television adaptations that Wuthering Heights has had over the years and, by making the choices that she did, she has taken this and set it completely in its own world. I definitely enjoyed watching what ended up being an extremely original and heartbreaking film of what happens when love becomes the pure essence of toxic. I highly recommend this film for anybody looking for a love story that can’t be put in a category and riveting performances, not just by the two leads, but by supporting cast, Hong Chau in particular.

This got me right in the Wutherussy. I am not a fan of the book. I read it ages ago, and then revisited it via Michael Ian Black’s Obscure podcast (seriously, if you love classic books and have a sense of humor, it’s such fun). This movie remembers that Heathcliff and Cathy are terrible people, and everyone suffers being near them. I really appreciated that. But more than that, it’s just swoony, horny, over the top, passionate indulgence. Yes, it takes liberties with the story, and I could take issue with several of the choices made (having just had it retold to me by MIB, I was more familiar than I would have been otherwise). Every movie version has and does. And the “Heathcliff is supposed to be Romany or Black” thing is also annoying: we know. Shut up. This version worked on me. I was gasping when I was supposed to gasp, and I got teary eyed at the end. It’s a bodice ripper, and it delivers.

Despite its Yorkshire filming location, most of the outdoor scenes seemed to have been shot in studio: the moors held more fog, more wind, and more thunder than nature would likely dispense and distracted from the action. The indoor scenes were saturated with bright colors, as if the director was determined to prove that more is better in every aspect. Including sex. There was A LOT of sex between Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi but about as much chemistry as between oil and water. One could almost hear the director’s prompts as they held each other and maneuvered to achieve certain so-called romantic images. When Elordi (I refuse to call him Heathcliff) repeated over and over “I love you”, he seemed to be attempting to convince himself. And what of his diction? For much of the movie, he appeared to have kept his morning porridge in his mouth. Robbie fared no better, especially when garbed in ridiculously outlandish gowns, another of the film’s many gratuitous distractions. As if she could not escape her role as Barbie, she was even given a dream house and bedroom, though both contained grotesque elements of decor. The only redeeming elements to this lurid spectacle were the supporting actors: two endearing children acted better than their adult counterparts, a conflicted lady in waiting instilled some sense of normalcy in this otherwise unhinged retelling, and a drunken father gave out a Dickensian aura. Not only did the director dare to cut away half the novel, but she also did away with several key characters leaving her leads deprived of their potential depth. It was a train wreck of an adaptation but as human nature would have it, most people will not be able to look away.

The movie is beautiful, visually dynamic and deeply emotional.  

Jacob Elordi's and Margot Robbie's portrayals of Heathcliff and Catherine show a deep respect and understanding of the essence of the characters. Alison Oliver was perfect as Isabella Linton. Charlotte Mellington (young Catherine) and Owen Cooper (young Heathcliff)  were amazing in their roles.

Although it does not follow Emily Bronte's book directly, what the movie does well is provide a deeper view of Catherine and Heathcliff's childhood friendship and experiences that shape who they will become as adults.  I felt more connected to the characters from the original story. 

However, the movie contains a few major detours from the original story. One character is left without closure to his story. Other characters are eliminated.  As I was reconnected to the characters from the book, I was left confused by the changes to their fates in the movie. 

The book transcends the “typical” ending. The movie provides, in some ways, the expected ending.

Ultimately,  this movie is a beautiful ”visual” interpretation of Catherine, Heathcliff and the moors.  For those who have not read the book, the ending will make sense.  For those who love Emily Bronte's story, put it aside before entering the theater.

Oh my sweaty stars, Emerald Fennell’s Wuthering Heights is a MASTERPIECE! It’s visually otherworldly. Every single frame is stunning, and Margot Robbie & Jacob Elordi positively ate their roles, delivering sublime performances steeped in piping hot chemistry.

As a fan of the Emily Brontë novel, I wasn’t sure going in whether the film would do the source material justice, but it was a more faithful adaptation than I expected. Through her creative lens, Fennell was able to drill down into the core of Cathy and Heathcliff and their toxic love story, and she found new layers and meaning to explore by reading between the lines. It’s also the only film adaptation of Wuthering Heights I’ve seen that accurately pegs the true villain of the story…FINALLY👏

While the pacing could have been tightened a bit in the middle—there's quite a stretch where Elordi is nowhere to be seen and many a forlorn stare from Robbie—it didn’t deter from the overall impact of the film or how much I enjoyed it. This is a singularly fresh, powerful, deeply emotional, and delightfully spicy take on a beloved classic, one I'll be thinking about for days/weeks/possibly forever. 4.75/5 stars
